title: Electronics Resale Arbitrage with Post-Delivery Chargeback Timing
narrative: Cardholder purchases high-resale electronics ($800-$2,500 range: laptops, gaming consoles, smartphones) from legitimate retailers, receives items at verified shipping address, then resells on secondary marketplaces within 5-10 days. After securing buyer payment through peer-to-peer apps or marketplace platforms, cardholder initiates dispute 12-21 days post-purchase claiming "item not received" or "defective product." Transaction shows all legitimate authorization signals (AVS match, CVV pass, device_id consistent with 3-6 month history), making it indistinguishable from genuine purchase at transaction time. Pattern emerges when cross-referencing: same card1 has 2-4 similar high-value electronics purchases across different merchants within 45-day windows, each followed by disputes 14-25 days later. Delivery confirmation exists but cardholder exploits merchant policies favoring customer claims. ProductCD shows "W" (electronics) exclusively. This represents intentional purchase-to-liquidate fraud disguised as legitimate consumer protection.
indicators: TransactionAmt between $800-$2,500 with ProductCD='W' and specific MCCs 5732 (electronics) or 5946 (camera stores)
card1_txn_count_30day shows 2-4 transactions in similar amount range ($750-$2,600) across different merchants
Dispute initiation timestamp 12-21 days post-transaction, consistently after standard delivery window (7-10 days)
All transactions show addr1 consistency and dist1 < 15km (verified shipping address pattern)
Device fingerprint (device_id) matches 85%+ of historical legitimate transactions on account (3-6 month lookback)
No failed authorization attempts or velocity flags at transaction time; AVS_match='Y', CVV_match='M'
Secondary marketplace activity detected through linked P_emaildomain or phone number appearing in resale platform data feeds within 5-10 days post-delivery
summary: A cardholder buys expensive electronics that pass all verification checks, receives delivery at their verified address, then files disputes 12-21 days later claiming non-receipt while the same card shows 2-4 similar high-value purchases across different merchants within 45 days.
reasoning: The fraudster is exploiting merchant chargeback policies to get refunds after already reselling the items for cash on secondary marketplaces, timing disputes after delivery confirmation but within dispute windows to double-dip on merchandise value.
indicator_explanations: High-value electronics purchases consistently in the resale sweet spot range
Cardholder repeatedly buys similar expensive items from multiple electronics retailers
Disputes filed precisely after delivery completes but before chargeback deadline
All purchases ship to verified home address with tight geographic clustering
Buyer uses their normal trusted device showing established legitimate account history
Clean authorizations with perfect security checks and no suspicious velocity patterns
Same email or phone appears selling electronics on resale platforms post-delivery
